death.cause: Year only; month/day approximate per project convention. Source: 'died 1761 aged 63.'

narrative_blocks.header: She and her sisters all married Hart brothers
narrative_blocks.body: She was one of three daughters of John Hooker Esq. of Farmington who each married a son of Capt. John Hart — Abigail to Nathaniel, Mary to Samuel, and Sarah to Matthew — between 1719 and 1725, uniting the two most prominent families of Farmington's founding generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Three sons served the Revolution differently
narrative_blocks.body: Her eldest son Selah rose to General and was captured by the British in 1776; Noadiah served as Lieutenant; and Asahel, Yale-educated and ordained as a minister, died in 1775 at age 33 before the war's outcome was known. Three brothers from one Hooker-Hart household, three paths through the Revolution.

cross_connections.display_label: Brother-in-law via Hart-Hooker triple marriage, 1719-1726
cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030) married three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033). Lieut. Samuel Hart married Abigail Hooker's sister Mary Hooker (H00100) in 1723; Abigail Hooker married I00028 in 1719.

cross_connections.display_label: Brother-in-law via Hart-Hooker triple marriage, 1719-1726
cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030) married three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033). Matthew Hart married Abigail Hooker's sister Sarah Hooker (H00101) in 1726; Abigail Hooker married I00028 in 1719.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her sisters all married Hart brothers
narrative_blocks.body: She was one of three daughters of John Hooker Esq. of Farmington who each married a son of Capt. John Hart — Abigail to Nathaniel, Mary to Samuel, and Sarah to Matthew — between 1719 and 1725, uniting the two most prominent families of Farmington's founding generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Three sons served the Revolution differently
narrative_blocks.body: Her eldest son Selah rose to General and was captured by the British in 1776; Noadiah served as Lieutenant; and Asahel, Yale-educated and ordained as a minister, died in 1775 at age 33 before the war's outcome was known. Three brothers from one Hooker-Hart household, three paths through the Revolution.

Samuel Hart married Abigail Hooker's sister Mary Hooker (H00100) in 1723; Abigail Hooker married I00028 in 1719.

parents.father_research_notes: Capt. John Hart (b. Apr 2, 1655, d. Nov 11, 1714)

cross_connections.display_label: Sister-in-law via Hart-Hooker triple marriage, 1719-1726
cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033) married three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030). Nathaniel Hart married I00028's wife's sister Mary Hooker (H00100, b.~1699); she had married Lieut. Samuel Hart in 1723.

cross_connections.display_label: Sister-in-law via Hart-Hooker triple marriage, 1719-1726
cross_connections.notes: One of the three Hart-Hooker marriages between 1719 and 1726: three sons of Capt. John Hart (X00033) married three daughters of Hon. John Hooker (H00030). Nathaniel Hart married I00028's wife's sister Sarah Hooker (H00101, b.~1701); she had married Matthew Hart in 1726.

narrative_blocks.header: Three Hart brothers each married a Hooker sister
narrative_blocks.body: He was one of three sons of Capt. John Hart who each married a daughter of John Hooker Esq. of Farmington — Nathaniel married Abigail, Samuel married Mary, and Matthew married Sarah, between 1719 and 1725, binding two of Farmington's founding families.

sources.notes: Memoir of Dr. Hurlbut (contains some errors per Hurlbut genealogy)

narrative_blocks.header: Called a father of medicine in Connecticut
narrative_blocks.body: He practiced widely in Kensington, Berlin, and Wethersfield from the 1740s through the 1790s. Hinman's Early Connecticut Settlers ranked him high in his profession.

narrative_blocks.header: Drink erased his name from the church
narrative_blocks.body: He fell into intemperance in middle age and the church removed him from membership. He had been careless in money matters his whole life, often not billing for his services.

narrative_blocks.header: Emma Willard remembered him from her childhood
narrative_blocks.body: Decades after his death she wrote that 'Dr. Hurlbut must have been a remarkable man, since he impressed himself so vividly upon the generation who came after him.' He had been her mother's physician.

narrative_blocks.header: Days into marriage, high words and parting
narrative_blocks.body: After his first wife Abigail Hart died, he married the widow of Robert Booth of Glastonbury late in life. Within days the union dissolved into 'high words' and a brief unhappy alliance.

narrative_blocks.header: Died in rags, buried with no marker
narrative_blocks.body: Cancer of the leg killed him in April 1794 at a friend's house in Wethersfield. He was buried near the north wall of Wethersfield churchyard with no monument.

military_service.role: Served approximately seven years, mostly as a teamster in transportation trains. Called 'Captain.' His first wife Sarah Hubbard died in Wallingford CT while Raphael was away with the army — their son Agathus was about 2 months old at the time.

narrative_blocks.header: Wife died with infant while he served
narrative_blocks.body: Raphael Hurlbut's first wife Sarah Hubbard died in Wallingford Connecticut in 1776 while Raphael was away with the Continental Army. Their son Agathus was about two months old at the time. The single-event combination — wife's home death, infant son, husband absent on extended military service — captured the personal cost of the Revolutionary War to families left behind on the Connecticut homefront.

narrative_blocks.header: Seven years Continental Army teamster, called Captain
narrative_blocks.body: Raphael Hurlbut served approximately seven years in the Continental Army — one of the longer single-soldier tours among Connecticut-line Hooker descendants. He served mostly as a teamster in the transportation trains, the supply-line logistics work that was distinct from frontline combat but was just as essential to the army's operations. He was called 'Captain' by his contemporaries.

narrative_blocks.header: Connecticut to Ohio migration 1812, died Springfield
narrative_blocks.body: Raphael lived first at Wallingford Connecticut, then at Salisbury Connecticut, and in 1812 removed to Springfield Ohio where he died June 16, 1822 around age seventy-eight. His second wife Abigail Riley Knott (widow of Giles Nott) later married John Hamilton in Ohio after Raphael's death. His father Dr. James Hurlbut, one of the founders of Connecticut medicine, had lived with him in Wallingford in his final years before returning to die in Wethersfield Connecticut.

military_service.notes: Trumpeter in Col. Sheldon's regiment. d. of consumption during the war. Prob. same man who d. Middletown CT, Mar 7, 1782.

cross_connections.display_label: Emma Willard acknowledged the Hurlbut-Hart family connection in an 1862 letter

narrative_blocks.header: First cousin to educator Emma Hart Willard
narrative_blocks.body: Philomela Hurlbut was a first cousin to Emma Hart Willard (1787-1870) — the pioneering women's-education advocate who founded the Troy Female Seminary in 1821, one of the first higher-education institutions for women in the United States. Emma later acknowledged the dual Hooker-line connection running through both the Hart and Hurlbut branches of their shared ancestry, the two lines converging through the marriage that united the Hurlbut and Hinsdale families.

narrative_blocks.header: Married her father's patient's brother
narrative_blocks.body: Philomela married John Hinsdale of Connecticut, brother of Lydia Hinsdale Hart — Lydia having been Philomela's father Dr. James Hurlbut's patient early in life. The doctor-patient connection that brought the Hurlbut and Hinsdale families into orbit produced the next-generation marriage between Philomela and John, with John being Lydia's brother and therefore Philomela's eventual brother-in-law via the Hart-Hinsdale connection.

narrative_blocks.header: Born Farmington CT 1754, died New Britain 1790
narrative_blocks.body: Philomela Hurlbut was born about 1754 in Farmington Connecticut and died December 22, 1790 in New Britain Connecticut at about age thirty-six. The short life — about thirty-six years — was typical of late-eighteenth-century childbearing-age mortality in colonial-and-Republic Connecticut. She is the documentary link between the Hurlbut and Hinsdale families of the Hooker tree.

military_service.notes: Hurlbut Genealogy entry 326: "a Rev. soldier, no further authentic particulars."

narrative_blocks.header: Nathaniel died young
narrative_blocks.body: Nathaniel died in 1728, in the first year. Nathaniel’s parents gave the name to a child born seven years later.

sources.short: Record of Service of Connecticut Men in the War of the Revolution, 1889
Berlin-Peck: Revolutionary War Soldiers of the Town of Berlin

cross_connections.display_label: His son Dr. William married my daughter Amelia in 1814.
commanded the company he guarded Boston with in early 1776

military_service.unit: Col. Erastus Wolcott's Regt. (Capt. Selah Hart's 3rd Co.); Gen. Wadsworth's Brigade; Maj. Sheldon's Light Horse (Capt. Daniel Sloper's Co.)
military_service.notes: 1776 campaign across three commands. Winter 1776: guarded besieged Boston about six weeks in Capt. Selah Hart's 3rd Company, Col. Erastus Wolcott's Regiment. Summer 1776: present at the Battle of Long Island (Aug 27) with Gen. James Wadsworth's Brigade and the retreat from New York. Nov 4–Dec 24, 1776: Corporal in Capt. Daniel Sloper's Company, Maj. Sheldon's Regiment of Light Horse, on Washington's retreat through New Jersey. Source: Record of Service of Connecticut Men in the War of the Revolution (1889), pp. 381, 392, 475; Berlin-Peck, Revolutionary War Soldiers of the Town of Berlin.

narrative_blocks.header: Married three times, his last two wives widows
narrative_blocks.body: After Ann Hooker died in 1808 he married twice more, both times to widows: Sabra Winchell Stanley, and in 1822 Abigail Hart Smith, herself already twice-widowed. His will, presented in court July 1827, left the farm and stock to his son Seth and the remainder to his daughters Lydia, Nancy, and Amelia.

narrative_blocks.header: His whole war fit in one brutal year
narrative_blocks.body: Seth's Revolutionary service was crowded into 1776. That year he helped guard besieged Boston through the winter, stood with the army in its rout at the Battle of Long Island, and rode in the light horse covering Washington's retreat across New Jersey.

military_service.unit: Col. Enos and Capt. Bray, CT State Regiment

sources.short: Slavery in Berlin — Forgotten No Longer

burial.plot_notes: Ledge Cemetery, Berlin, Hartford County, Connecticut

H00273.prominent_name_reason: General Selah Hart (1732-1806) — Brigadier General, Connecticut Militia. Lt. Col. 15th Regiment under Col. Fisher Gay; captured at Battle of Long Island Aug 27, 1776. Held prisoner ~2 years while wife Ruth knew not if he lived. Promoted to Colonel in captivity after Gay's death. Appointed Brigadier General, 6th Brigade of Militia 1779. Delegate to State Convention for ratification of US Constitution, 1788. Post-war: magistrate, constable, deacon, treasurer, general of CT Militia for many years. "Patron Saint of Connecticut" per later sources. Monument in Farmington Cemetery. Slave owner: Jack and Pharaoh Hart. Pharaoh served at Germantown (1777) and Monmouth (1778); Hart demanded all of Pharaoh's army wages as compensation for freeing him. Jack freed voluntarily at 21 without payment. See: https://digitalfarmington.org/digital-farmington-project/selah-hart-saint-and-sinner/

political_offices.office: Delegate, CT State Convention for US Constitution Ratification

enslaved_persons.name: Jack
enslaved_persons.description: Taken as a very young child. Freed voluntarily at age 21 without payment. Hart told him: "The law would give me four years more of your service, but I think you have the right to be free." Received education and clothing better than average white lads in the vicinity.
enslaved_persons.source: History of New Britain; Berlin slavery article 2022

enslaved_persons.name: Pharaoh
enslaved_persons.description: Allowed to enlist in Continental Army for 3 years on condition of paying Hart a portion of wages for freedom. Fought at Battle of Germantown PA (1777) and Monmouth NJ (1778). At end of service, Hart claimed ALL wages — not just a portion — citing his own imprisonment debt. Pharaoh had no choice but to pay. 35 years later, both Pharaoh and Selah Hart received pensions. In 1777, Selah sat in a British prison while Pharaoh fought for American freedom.
enslaved_persons.source: Digital Farmington / Menapace 2022; Berlin slavery article

notable.notable_blurb: Revolutionary brigadier general, prisoner of war, slaveholder

cross_connections.display_label: Managed this mine for the Revolution — Percival surveyed it as geologist
First cousins — both commanded Connecticut militia in the Revolution.
Adopted nephew who inherited the General Hart homestead
cross_connections.notes: Cyprian Hart (H00562, son of Selah's brother Nathaniel Hart No. 2) was orphaned of his father at age 4 in 1773. The childless Selah adopted him as heir and raised him at the Kensington homestead. Cyprian m. Lucy Hooker (H00537) Dec 24, 1795 and died fourteen weeks before his uncle on March 1, 1806. His widow Lucy and Selah's widow Ruth Cole Hart kept the General Hart house together for 38 years.

cross_connections.display_label: Great-niece whose marriage carried the homestead out of Hart hands
cross_connections.notes: Abigail Hart (H01446, daughter of his adopted nephew Cyprian Hart) m. Oliver Moore March 25, 1829. Per Hart Genealogy, the former General Hart homestead became the Moore residence by the time of Abigail's death in 1850 — three-generation property descent ending the Hart family ownership: Gen. Selah Hart → adopted nephew Cyprian Hart → daughter Abigail → husband Oliver Moore.

cross_connections.display_label: served in his Revolutionary militia company
served in his company guarding Boston in early 1776

narrative_blocks.header: Captured retreating from New York — held two years
narrative_blocks.body: He was captured on September 15, 1776 while retreating from New York City, and held prisoner for approximately two years. His enslaved man Pharaoh was allowed to enlist and earn his freedom in his absence — Pharaoh fought at Germantown in 1777 and at Monmouth in 1778; thirty-five years later both Hart and Pharaoh received federal pensions.

narrative_blocks.header: Washington breakfasted with him
narrative_blocks.body: On November 10, 1789, President Washington breakfasted in Berlin while touring Connecticut. Hart greeted him and later recorded that he was 'too full for utterance.' His wife Ruth Cole lived to approximately age 101, outliving him by 38 years.

narrative_blocks.header: He freed enslaved Jack four years early
narrative_blocks.body: "The law would give me four years more of your service, but I think you have the right to be free," he told Jack. He also gave Pharaoh the chance to earn freedom through military service — an unusual act for a Connecticut officer of his era.

narrative_blocks.header: He took every shilling a freedman earned
narrative_blocks.body: Selah promised Pharaoh freedom for three years in the Continental Army while collecting part of his wages as compensation. When the term ended, Selah — in debt from his imprisonment — demanded the entirety. Pharaoh, who had fought at Germantown and Monmouth for a freedom he had never known, had no choice but to pay.

cross_connections.display_label: Adopted nephew's widow; shared the house thirty-eight years
cross_connections.notes: After husband General Selah Hart died June 10, 1806 and his adopted nephew Cyprian Hart died fourteen weeks earlier on March 1, 1806, Ruth (then 63) and Cyprian's widow Lucy Hooker Hart (then 34) kept the General Hart house together for the rest of Ruth's life. Ruth died there January 15, 1844 at 101 years, 2 months, 16 days. EH #273.

narrative_blocks.header: The oldest person who ever died in Kensington
narrative_blocks.body: Ruth outlived Selah by thirty-eight years, dying at one hundred one years, two months, and sixteen days — the oldest person who ever died in Kensington. Her tombstone reads that “her memory will triumph over the wrecks of time.”

death.notes: Only child of Gen. Selah Hart by first wife; died young per EH; 1752 estimated

narrative_blocks.header: Mary died young
narrative_blocks.body: Mary died young; the dates were not recorded.

military_service.notes: Rank 'Major' per EH 1909; war, unit, and dates unresearched.

narrative_blocks.header: A Vermont detour, then back to Farmington
narrative_blocks.body: After marrying, Gideon moved his growing family north to Castleton, Vermont, only to turn back and resettle in Farmington. So thoroughly did the household uproot and return that even the birthplaces of his children were later left in doubt.

narrative_blocks.header: Fatherless young, the family left Kensington
narrative_blocks.body: When Gideon was still a boy, his father died and his widowed mother moved the household from Kensington to Hartford. He came of age in the shadow of that early loss before building a restless, much-relocated life of his own.

narrative_blocks.header: Two wives lost, a third marriage, then gone
narrative_blocks.body: Seth married three times in fifteen years. Anna died in 1799, the widow Lydia he married next died in 1812, and after a third marriage that same year he was himself dead within eighteen months.

burial.plot_notes: Tombstone in Blue Hills Cemetery (per Hart Genealogy).

sources.short: Andrews, Genealogical History of Deacon Stephen Hart (1875)
Andrews, Genealogical History of Deacon Stephen Hart (1875)

death.cause: Killed by fall of tree (or limb of tree per Hart Genealogy)

naming_inspiration.description: Named for his uncle Gen. Selah Hart of Kensington — the same uncle who later adopted his orphaned brother Cyprian (H00562) in 1773 and raised him at the General Hart homestead.

narrative_blocks.header: Settled at Marietta on the Ohio frontier
narrative_blocks.body: Fifth son of Nathaniel Hart No. 2, named for his uncle General Selah Hart of Kensington who had adopted his brother Cyprian. Migrated from Connecticut to the Ohio frontier — among the early settlers at Marietta, Washington County, in the Ohio Company's territory. EH #561 records his Ohio descendants at numbers 1494-1503.

narrative_blocks.header: Killed by a falling tree in Ohio, 1836
narrative_blocks.body: He died in Ohio in 1836, killed by a falling tree. One son Norman had been killed earlier at Sandusky in 1813 during the War of 1812. EH numbers ten Ohio-born descendants in the next generation.

narrative_blocks.header: Fourteen years a magistrate of excellent judgment
narrative_blocks.body: He served as a magistrate at Marion, Morgan County, Ohio for about fourteen years, and was generally accounted of good judgment and local influence. Both he and his wife Sarah were active church members from early life. The Hart Genealogy of 1875 records that the place was greatly missed and mourned over at his death, so great was the loss.

sources.short: Andrews, Genealogical History of Deacon Stephen Hart (1875)

burial.plot_notes: Ledge Cemetery, Berlin CT. FindAGrave Memorial #61935571.

cross_connections.display_label: Connecticut Militia General who took him in at four
cross_connections.notes: Cyprian's father Nathaniel Hart No. 2 died October 13, 1773 when Cyprian was four. His childless uncle Gen. Selah Hart (whose only natural child Mary had died young) adopted him as heir to the General Hart homestead. Cyprian died March 1, 1806; Selah died fourteen weeks later on June 10, 1806.

cross_connections.display_label: Two Cyprian Harts, both adopted by Gen. Selah Hart
cross_connections.notes: H00562 (this entry) and H00568 both bore the name Cyprian Hart and both were documented as adopted heirs of General Selah Hart (H00273) — a striking naming + adoption coincidence.

narrative_blocks.header: Father dead at four, raised on uncle's homestead
narrative_blocks.body: When Nathaniel Hart died at Kensington on October 13, 1773, his sixth son Cyprian was four years old. Widowed Martha Norton soon removed to Hartford, and the boy was taken in by his childless uncle General Selah Hart and raised on the General Hart homestead as adopted heir.

narrative_blocks.header: Married Lucy Hooker at twenty-three, both born 1772
narrative_blocks.body: On December 24, 1795 he married Lucy Hooker of the same Kensington parish, born four months and ten days after him in January 1772. They were second cousins through their shared great-grandfather, the Honorable John Hooker of Farmington. Six children arrived in nine years.

narrative_blocks.header: Died before the General who had raised him
narrative_blocks.body: He died at Kensington on March 1, 1806, aged thirty-seven, leaving his youngest daughter Emily nine months old. General Selah Hart followed him into the grave fourteen weeks later, on June 10, in the same house. By the adoption arrangement the General Hart estate passed through his nephew's six children rather than to Selah's collateral kin.

narrative_blocks.header: Two widows shared the house thirty-eight years
narrative_blocks.body: After both husbands died in the spring of 1806, his widow Lucy Hooker Hart and his adoptive aunt Ruth Cole Hart kept the Kensington homestead together for the rest of their lives. Ruth died in the house in January 1844 at one hundred and one. Lucy lived another seven years alone, dying there in July 1851.

narrative_blocks.header: Lieutenant while his brother Selah was a General
narrative_blocks.detail: The Hart family of Kensington had deep military roots: their father Nathaniel Hart (I00028) had married Abigail Hooker in 1719 in one of the three simultaneous Hart-Hooker marriages. A third brother, Rev. Asahel Hart (H00276, Yale 1764), became the first pastor of North Canaan Congregational Church — the three Hart brothers who survived to adulthood representing military service, Congregationalist ministry, and civic life respectively. Noadiah was appointed administrator of the estate of Jonathan Hurlbut Jr. in October 1761, confirming his standing in the Kensington community. (EH 1909; Hart Genealogy 1948)
narrative_blocks.body: Noadiah Hart served as a Lieutenant in the American Revolutionary War while his older brother Selah Hart rose to General in the Connecticut Militia — captured by the British in September 1776 during the New York retreat. Both were sons of Nathaniel Hart × Abigail Hooker, 4th-generation descendants of Thomas Hooker. Noadiah served through the war, became a Deacon in the Harwinton church by 1804, and died in Southington in 1817 at 80.

narrative_blocks.header: Deacon of Harwinton church; lived to 80
narrative_blocks.body: After the war, he became Deacon of the church in Harwinton in 1804, removed to Southington, and lived in his daughter Velina's household near her mill — a site later covered by the "Plant Ponds." He died in 1817 at age 80, the longest-lived of the three brothers.

sources.short: Andrews, Genealogical History of Deacon Stephen Hart (1875)

bio.bio_blurb: Southington miller at the mill now under Plant's Pond

burial.plot_notes: Tombstone in Plantsville Cemetery (per Hart Genealogy).

narrative_blocks.header: He ran the mill now under Plant's Pond
narrative_blocks.body: Velina Hart was a miller at Southington, running what became Carter's Mill; the mill and his house are long gone, the site now covered by Plant's Pond.

narrative_blocks.header: Stephen died young
narrative_blocks.body: Stephen died in 1766, in the first year. Stephen’s parents gave the name to a child born one year later.

sources.short: Andrews, Genealogical History of Deacon Stephen Hart (1875)
Andrews, Genealogical History of Deacon Stephen Hart (1875)

burial.plot_notes: Chapel Hill Cemetery, Parishville, St. Lawrence County, NY

cross_connections.display_label: Two Cyprian Harts, both adopted by Gen. Selah Hart
cross_connections.notes: H00568 (this entry) and H00562 both bore the name Cyprian Hart and both were documented as adopted heirs of General Selah Hart (H00273) — a striking naming + adoption coincidence.

cross_connections.display_label: owned the New Britain mill Cyprian ran

bio.bio_blurb: New Britain miller, later of Parishville

narrative_blocks.header: He ran Deacon Elijah Hart's mill
narrative_blocks.body: A miller, Cyprian Hart came to New Britain to run Deacon Elijah Hart's mill and raised his family beside it before removing to Parishville, New York.

career.organization: Deacon Elijah Hart's mill, New Britain

bio.notes: Named for deceased mother Lucy Hurlburt Hart (d.1785)

narrative_blocks.header: Lucy died young
narrative_blocks.body: Lucy died in 1795 at the age of nine.

church_roles.location: Second Church of Christ, North Canaan, Connecticut
church_roles.notes: Died in 6th year of ministry, age 33

narrative_blocks.header: Yale 1764 — first pastor of North Canaan
narrative_blocks.body: He graduated Yale in 1764, organized the North Canaan Congregational Church on December 5, 1769, and was installed as its first pastor on March 14, 1770. He died unmarried on June 28, 1775 — at 33 years old, just as the Revolutionary War was beginning, two of his brothers about to serve.

sources.full: L.J. Hooker, 'Edward Hooker, Commander, U.S. Navy,' The Old Northwest Genealogical Quarterly (Columbus, Ohio, 1898-), VII, 12

cross_connections.notes: Col. Wadsworth's son Daniel Wadsworth founded the Wadsworth Atheneum in Hartford — a monument with Hooker family kinship interest. Research needed to confirm Mehitable Russell's Hooker descent line. Warren 1887 confirms: Elizabeth Standley married John Wadsworth — their son Daniel Wadsworth founded the Wadsworth Atheneum.
cross_connections.display_label: was his father-in-law, honored in the Indian wars

cross_connections.notes: Capt. Thomas Hart b. ca. 1644, d. Aug 27, 1726 age 82. Son of Stephen Hart (X00034). Brother of John Hart who survived the 1666 fire (X00033). Not in this tree through marriage — noted as civic context only.
cross_connections.display_label: helped govern Farmington alongside him for decades
married a descendant of both Hooker and Standley
served Hartford’s founding-family public life alongside him
was his wife’s sister and Col. Jeremiah Wadsworth’s grandmother
fathered four children who married four of his own
Both served as Connecticut Colony Supreme Court judges.
was the cousin whose Indian Neck deed he registered
signed a Tunxis dispossession deed as his descendant’s in-law
shared his entanglement in Tunxis affairs, decades apart
was called with him one of Farmington’s two prominent men

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington, Hartford County, Connecticut. Same cemetery as his father Rev. Samuel Hooker (H00007).

notable.primary_url_label: Native Northeast Portal — John Hooker biography

narrative_blocks.header: Judge of Connecticut Colony's Supreme Court
narrative_blocks.body: He served as Judge of the Supreme Court of Connecticut Colony from 1724 to 1732 and as Speaker of the Lower House. He also served as witness, certifying Assistant, and Registrar in the 1733 Sue Squa land deed — three official roles in a single transaction.

narrative_blocks.header: He defended Tunxis land rights in court
narrative_blocks.body: In 1738, John Hooker provided a formal statement to the Connecticut General Court that ninety acres at Indian Neck had 'always belonged' to the Tunxis tribe — an unusual act of testimony on behalf of the indigenous people whose land his own ancestors had settled near.

narrative_blocks.header: The elm fell so someone could walk straight
narrative_blocks.body: His father gave him a site on the west side of Farmington's main street, where he built under the branches of a great elm. The tree became famous through all the region for the company that gathered beneath it — three generations of Hookers lived in its shade before a later owner cut it down to straighten the sidewalk.

narrative_blocks.header: The bell tolled — Hooker stepped out first
narrative_blocks.body: A chronicler recorded that every Sunday the bell rang, then began tolling; at the first stroke, Hooker issued from his house and proceeded to the Meeting House. The minister walked from his house. Whoever arrived first waited at the door; together they entered — at which the bell stopped and all the congregation arose.

narrative_blocks.header: The Sabbath ended when Hooker touched his gate
narrative_blocks.body: The holiness of Sunday was supposed to end at sunset — but no child in the town dared stir until John Hooker gave the signal. When the sun sank, he walked to his gate and leaned on it; open flew the doors and out came the children with a wild rush and shout.

narrative_blocks.header: Dark hair, dark eyes — dignity commanding respect
narrative_blocks.body: His grandson described him: "Dark hair and dark eyes, and a dignity in his air and expression that procured profound respect." A fine looking man, tall, very erect, and had a prominent large nose. He lived his entire life in Farmington, dying there in 1746 at age 81.

career.notes: Served as justice and register of Farmington
From Farmington. Served as Clerk two sessions; Speaker six sessions.
Served as judge of Superior Court concurrent with assistant role
Commissioner of the Mohegan's sequestered land at New London

cross_connections.display_label: Both married Hooker brothers — from two separate Stanley families.
Their families united — three daughters married Hart brothers.
Her sister Elizabeth Standley married into the Wadsworth line.

narrative_blocks.header: Her father won distinction in the Indian wars
narrative_blocks.body: Capt. John Standley was a man of wealth and high social position who had served as lieutenant and captain in the Indian wars. Her sister Elizabeth Standley's granddaughter married Col. Jeremiah Wadsworth, Commissary General of the Continental Army — Wadsworth's wife also descended from Rev. Thomas Hooker, making Wadsworth's children doubly connected to the Hooker line.

bio.notes: Also spelled 'Stanley' in EH entry 30; 'Standley' in entries 99-101

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

No stone survives. Well-attested tradition places the grave at the south side of Rev. Samuel Whitman's marked grave. "Mr. Whitman like Mr. Hooker, died while the minister of the town, and tradition says he was buried by the side of Mr. Hooker."

sources.notes: John Winthrop Jr., Alchemy and the Creation of New England Culture, 1606-1676 (Homer Babbidge Prize)

narrative_blocks.header: He inherited his father's library — and his calling
narrative_blocks.body: Thomas Hooker's will gave his library to John on condition that John pursue ministry; if John faltered, the entire library and manuscripts went to Samuel. John did not become a minister. Samuel received his father's books — and then dedicated his own life to the ministry his father had hoped for.

narrative_blocks.header: Prosecutor in the Hartford witch trials
narrative_blocks.body: In the 1660s he formed a prosecutorial tribunal with Rev. Samuel Stone of Hartford and Rev. Joseph Haynes of Wethersfield. Connecticut tried witches more aggressively than Massachusetts in this decade — three decades before the Salem trials.

narrative_blocks.header: Ordered to King Philip's War, then quietly replaced
narrative_blocks.body: On March 9, 1675-6, the Council ordered Hooker to prepare to march into King Philip's War with Major Treat's army toward Hadley and Northampton. Two days later, the Council quietly replaced him with Rev. Israel Chauncy. No reason was recorded — only the substitution.

narrative_blocks.header: He persuaded Farmington to found a free school
narrative_blocks.body: In December 1685, the town of Farmington voted to give £30 for a schoolmaster for one year — and declared it a free school for the town, almost certainly at Hooker's instigation. It was among the earliest free public schools in colonial Connecticut.

narrative_blocks.header: The 'Fervent Hooker,' who preached from the heart
narrative_blocks.body: Rev. Pitkin described him as 'an excellent preacher, his composition good, his address pathetic, warm and engaging.' Hooker told a friend he had three things to do with his sermons before delivering them: write them, commit them to memory, and get them into his heart.

cross_connections.display_label: Five Hooker generations on the same Farmington ground.
Farmington's first pastor, whom Hooker succeeded
His grandfather Tobie Willet sailed with John Talcott on the Lion.
Her husband's Prudden line connects to his wife Mary Willet's family
was his father-in-law; First Mayor of New York City, 1665

quotes.text: Thus we have to this day among us our dead Hooker, yet living in his worthy son Samuel Hooker, an able, faithful, useful minister at Farmington, in the Colony of Connecticut.

education.notes: B.A. 1653. Entered Harvard 1651. Chosen Fellow of the College November 27, 1654.

burial.plot_notes: Near center of burying ground, near East Norwalk railway station

narrative_blocks.header: Daughter of New York City's first mayor
narrative_blocks.body: Her father Capt. Thomas Willet was appointed the first Mayor of New York City in 1665, when the English renamed the Dutch colony. He had previously succeeded Capt. Miles Standish as commander of Plymouth's militia — one of the most storied military commands in early New England. Mary carried this distinguished lineage to Farmington when she married Rev. Samuel Hooker.

narrative_blocks.header: Three generations of Willet women known for beauty
narrative_blocks.body: Mary's mother was described as possessing 'beauty and majesty almost divine.' A portrait of Mary's daughter Mary Hooker Pierpont survives, and from it contemporaries formed 'the opinion that she was a woman of uncommon beauty.' Her daughter Sarah was 'accounted the most accomplished lady that had ever come to Norwalk.'

narrative_blocks.header: Remarried at 66 — outlived two minister husbands
narrative_blocks.body: After Samuel Hooker's death in 1697, Mary married Reverend Thomas Buckingham of Saybrook at age 66. When he died in 1709 she moved to Norwalk to live with her son-in-law Reverend Stephen Buckingham, where she remained until her death in 1712 at age 74.

narrative_blocks.header: She and her daughter married father and son
narrative_blocks.body: After Rev. Samuel Hooker died, Mary Willet married Rev. Thomas Buckingham of Saybrook in 1703. Her daughter Sarah was the wife of his son, Rev. Stephen Buckingham, so a mother and daughter had married a father and his son.

cross_connections.display_label: Her grandfather Tobie Willet sailed to New England with John Talcott.
Her father Thomas Willet's widow's first husband was ancestor of H00153's husband

open_questions.question: Connection to Sir John Standley, officer in Cromwell's army
open_questions.notes: EH notes Sir John Standley was an officer in Cromwell's army and that the family was then an old one with no known connection to the famous English Stanley family. EH argues the Connecticut settlers spelled the name correctly as 'Standley' and the later standardization to 'Stanley' was a mistake. Research at Farmington Historical Society and English army records may confirm.
open_questions.action: Check Cromwell's army officer records for Sir John Standley; check Farmington Historical Society; compare with Stanley family pedigrees

sources.full: Israel P. Warren D.D., The Stanley Families of America as Descended from John, Timothy, and Thomas Stanley of Hartford, CT, 1636 (1887)

parents.father_research_notes: John Stanley, emigrant from England — settled Hartford 1636

political_offices.title: Deputy to General Court
political_offices.notes: Warren: 'almost continually for thirty-seven years — with few exceptions, the longest term of service known in the annals of the state'

cross_connections.notes: Abigail Stanley married John Hooker (H00030); Elizabeth Stanley married John Wadsworth (whose son Daniel founded Wadsworth Atheneum)

cross_connections.notes: Abigail Stanley married John Hooker (H00030); Elizabeth Stanley married John Wadsworth (whose son Daniel founded Wadsworth Atheneum)

notable.notable_blurb: Long-serving Farmington deputy and militia captain

narrative_blocks.header: He marched against the Pequots at age thirteen
narrative_blocks.body: John Stanley came to Hartford as a boy in his uncle's care. In 1637, only thirteen, he joined the colony's expedition against the Pequots, the year after the settlers arrived.

narrative_blocks.header: He served thirty-seven years as a deputy
narrative_blocks.body: From 1659 to 1696 Stanley represented Farmington at the General Court almost without a break, one of the longest terms of service in the colony's records.

narrative_blocks.header: He built the house that became Farmington's museum
narrative_blocks.body: Around 1665 Stanley built a home on the Farmington street for his second wife. Long known as the Stanley-Whitman House, it survives today as the town's museum.

narrative_blocks.header: He rose to captain in King Philip's War
narrative_blocks.body: During King Philip's War Stanley served as a lieutenant and then captain, the rank that gave him the name by which he was always known, Capt. John Stanley.

narrative_blocks.header: He married the same day as his sister
narrative_blocks.body: On December 5, 1645, John Stanley married Sarah Scott at Hartford. The same day his sixteen-year-old sister Ruth married Isaac Moore, and both young couples settled together at Farmington.

landmarks.landmark_blurb: He acquired the property where this house was later built.

military_service.notes: Warren: 'when a mere boy of thirteen, he went in the expedition against the Pequots, the very year after the settlers came to Hartford'
Lieutenant then Captain; received 120-acre land grant from General Court 1674 and another 1687

bio.title: Captain
bio.notes: Surname also spelled 'Stanley' — EH argues 'Standley' is the correct original spelling

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington CT; gravestone partly illegible. SOURCE: FindAGrave; Stanley Genealogy.

bio.bio_blurb: Second wife of Capt. John Stanley; mother of Abigail Hooker

burial.plot_notes: Memento Mori Cemetery, Farmington CT. SOURCE: FindAGrave.

narrative_blocks.header: Her husband built her the Stanley-Whitman House
narrative_blocks.body: About 1665 Capt. John Stanley built a home on the Farmington street for his second wife, Sarah. She raised his younger children there, among them Abigail, who married Hon. John Hooker.
